subject: TravCo Insurance Company Claim JEE8887 — Back-slope approved, front-slope denied (wear/tear) + masterful manufacturer-spec counter
adjuster: Kirkland Nelms, Property Claim Professional, Virginia-Carolina Claim Center, Travelers (W 571-623-9034, KNelms@travelers.com)
denialText: Attached is a copy of the approved estimate. The estimate is written to replace the entire back portion of the roof and the metal box accessories. The wind damage is what triggered the replacement, and since the back slope runs continuous to the left and right slopes on the reverse gable that's how I was able to get coverage for the entire back side of the roof rather than only the back slope. Since there was no wind damage to the front slope there was no coverage provided for replacement. The hail consideration to the shingles was determined to not be from hail, and instead was a result of granule loss and blistering.
counterText: Thank you for outlining the approved scope of repairs and the reasoning behind the current coverage determination. We appreciate the consideration given to replacing the full back portion of the roof due to wind damage and the continuity of the slopes along the reverse gable. After reviewing the approved estimate, I would like to raise a concern from a contractor standpoint regarding the exclusion of the front slope. Per Owens Corning VentSure® ridge vent installation requirements, any ridge-related work requires cutting, fastening, and working a minimum of 6 inches past ridge terminations and into the adjoining slope. This means that completing the approved back-slope replacement and ridge work cannot be performed in isolation without disturbing the shingles on the front slope. In order to install the ridge vent properly and in accordance with manufacturer specifications, shingles on both sides of the ridge must be lifted, fastened, and integrated into the system. From a construction and workmanship standpoint, proceeding under the current scope would require us to physically manipulate and potentially damage the front slope shingles during installation while leaving those same shingles in place afterward. This creates a risk of broken seals, compromised shingles, and future leaks, and it prevents us from warranting the work or ensuring compliance with manufacturer installation standards. Additionally, the same logic used to extend coverage to the left and right rear slopes—due to their continuous connection through the gable—also applies at the ridge. The ridge is a continuous transition between the back and front slopes, and work performed on one side inherently impacts the other. There is no method to properly complete the approved repairs without affecting the front slope at the ridge line. For these reasons, we respectfully request reconsideration of the scope to include the front slope so the roof can be restored properly, without introducing new damage, and in full compliance with manufacturer requirements and accepted roofing practices.
keyDenialLanguage: The hail consideration to the shingles was determined to not be from hail
instead was a result of granule loss and blistering
Since there was no wind damage to the front slope there was no coverage provided for replacement
since the back slope runs continuous to the left and right slopes on the reverse gable
carrierTactic: TRAVELERS WEAR-AND-TEAR PATTERN — classify granule loss + blistering as 'not hail' (the Accurence/Verisk patent-driven AI signature). Approve back slope + side slopes (since they're 'continuous'), deny front slope. The 'continuity' logic is INCONSISTENTLY APPLIED — it justifies coverage for adjacent back/side slopes, but NOT for the front slope connected to the same ridge.
rooferTactic: CONTRACTOR STANDPOINT COUNTER — Chris Aycock's response is template-quality. It (1) acknowledges carrier's good faith on back-slope coverage, (2) cites SPECIFIC manufacturer (Owens Corning VentSure) installation requirements requiring 6" past ridge terminations, (3) frames the request as RISK to homeowner (broken seals, future leaks) AND inability to warrant the work, (4) uses the carrier's OWN continuity logic (rear-gable connection) to extend to the ridge → front slope connection. Pure contractor-role argument, no public-adjuster overreach.
patentMapping: Travelers' wear-and-tear classifier (Accurence US10089396, Dolphin AI WO2018156846) labels 'granule loss + blistering' as 'not hail' default. The patent provides no mechanism to distinguish between THERMAL granule loss vs HAIL-INDUCED granule loss — Roof Docs counter cites manufacturer specs to bypass the AI classification entirely.
lessonForAnalyzer: When Travelers/TravCo denies a slope citing 'granule loss and blistering' wear-and-tear: counter-letter should (1) NOT argue the classification directly (you'll lose against patent-trained AI), (2) instead cite SHINGLE MANUFACTURER's installation requirements that REQUIRE work on the denied slope, (3) use carrier's own continuity logic against them (if back-to-side counts, ridge-to-front counts), (4) frame as contractor-warranty issue + future-leak risk, NOT a damage-classification dispute. Chris Aycock's letter is the gold-standard template for this pattern.
